---
filters:
  - jank
---

# Overview {.unnumbered}

**Janqua** is a [Quarto](https://quarto.org) extension for evaluating [Jank](https://jank-lang.org) code blocks in documents.

Write Jank code in `.qmd` files and get live results — code output, inline [SVG](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SVG), [Plotly.js](https://plotly.com/javascript/) charts, markdown tables, and more. Use it for publishing documents or for interactive data exploration with `quarto preview`, which re-renders on every save. Uses the [Kindly](https://scicloj.github.io/kindly-noted/) convention for rendering, compatible with the [Clojure](https://clojure.org/) ecosystem.

::: {.callout-note}
Janqua is at an **experimental stage**. Things may change, and some rough edges are expected. Feedback, bug reports, and ideas are very welcome — please open an issue on [GitHub](https://github.com/scicloj/janqua/issues) or reach out on the [Clojurians Zulip chat](https://scicloj.github.io/docs/community/chat/).
:::

## Status
experimental

## What is Quarto?

[Quarto](https://quarto.org) is an open-source publishing system for technical documents. You write `.qmd` (Quarto Markdown) files mixing prose and code, and Quarto renders them to HTML, PDF, slides, and other formats.

Quarto supports several project types:

- **Single documents** — a standalone `.qmd` file rendered to HTML
- **[Websites](https://quarto.org/docs/websites/)** — multiple pages with navigation, like a documentation site
- **[Books](https://quarto.org/docs/books/)** — chapters rendered as a navigable book (like this one)

## What is Jank?

[Jank](https://jank-lang.org) is a [Clojure](https://clojure.org/) dialect that compiles to native code via C++. It aims for full Clojure compatibility with the performance of a native runtime. Jank includes an [nREPL](https://nrepl.org/) server, which Janqua uses to evaluate code interactively during document rendering.

## Clojure ecosystem connection

[Clay](https://scicloj.github.io/clay) is a Clojure tool for data visualization and [literate programming](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Literate_programming). Clay generates Quarto markdown from Clojure namespaces, then uses Quarto as a rendering engine to produce HTML pages, books, and slideshows. [Clojure Civitas](https://clojurecivitas.org/), the community blog, is built this way. Civitas can also render `.qmd` files directly (not just Clay-generated ones), so Janqua documents are expected to work well with it.

Janqua brings a similar experience to Jank: you write Jank code directly in `.qmd` files, and the Quarto filter evaluates it during rendering. Both Janqua and Clay use the [Kindly](https://scicloj.github.io/kindly-noted/) convention for rendering annotations, so code patterns are transferable between the two ecosystems.

## How it works

1. You write a `.qmd` file with `{.jank}` code blocks
2. When Quarto renders the document, the Janqua filter intercepts these blocks
3. Each block is sent to a Jank nREPL server for evaluation
4. Results are inserted into the document — as code output, HTML, or markdown — based on [Kindly annotations](kindly.qmd)

```{.jank}
^:kind/hiccup
[:div {:style "color: coral; font-size: 24px;"}
  "Hello from Jank!"]
```

The Jank process starts automatically on first render and stays running for fast re-evaluation. See [Getting Started](getting-started.qmd) to try it out, or jump to the [Live Demo](example.qmd) to see evaluated examples.

## Future goals

- **[Clojure Civitas](https://clojurecivitas.org/) integration** — enabling community members to publish Jank posts on the blog
- **Broader Kindly support** — more visualization kinds (tables, [Vega-Lite](https://vega.github.io/vega-lite/), images, etc.)
- **Easier setup** — reducing the number of prerequisites and simplifying installation
- **[Clay](https://scicloj.github.io/clay) support for Jank** — a namespace-as-a-notebook experience, where Jank source files render as documents without writing `.qmd` by hand
- **Jupyter support** — a Jank kernel for Jupyter notebooks, enabling interactive Jank development in JupyterLab and other Jupyter-compatible environments

## Chapters

- [Getting Started](getting-started.qmd) — prerequisites, installation, first document, rendering
- [Kindly Annotations](kindly.qmd) — controlling output format with metadata
- [How It Works](how-it-works.qmd) — architecture, port discovery, process management, evaluation pipeline
- [Live Demo](example.qmd) — evaluated examples of all output kinds
